_general: Karlo's Observatory Console Type 'help [command]' for more information on a specific command.

help.description: Provides help information for console commands.
help.details:
Displays a list of all available commands grouped by category. If a command is specified, shows detailed information including usage, description, and examples.

Examples:
  help
  help boids
  help radio

bonsai.description: An idle bonsai tree that grows using the Space Colonization Algorithm, even while you're away.
bonsai.details:
A living bonsai tree that grows slowly over days. Each visit picks up where it left off — offline time is fast-forwarded on load.

Interaction:
  Click or tap any branch to prune it. Pruned branches release growth points back into the crown, letting the tree regrow in a new shape.
  Click or tap the pot to start over with a fresh tree.

The trunk and first branches grow quickly on first load. After that, growth slows to a natural pace — a full canopy takes a few days.

Example:
  bonsai

boids.description: A flocking simulation based on Craig Reynolds' Boids algorithm.
boids.details:
Simulates the collective motion of a group of boids. The simulation can be customized with the following options:

  -b, --boids       [int]    The desired number of boids to simulate (Default: 1024)
  -s, --separation  [float]  Strength of the force to avoid crowding (Default: 0.01)
  -a, --alignment   [float]  Strength of the force to steer towards the average flock direction (Default: 0.015)
  -c, --cohesion    [float]  Strength of the force to steer towards the center of the flock (Default: 0.6)

Use your mouse to apply a repulsive force to the boids.

Examples:
  boids
  boids -b 512
  boids -b 2048 -s 0.02 -a 0.01 -c 0.4

gameoflife.description: Conway's Game of Life, a cellular automaton.
gameoflife.details:
A zero-player game where the evolution of the system is determined by its initial state. Click or drag on the grid to draw living cells.

Example:
  gameoflife

evoltree.details:
Visualizes how species can diverge over time through reproduction and mutation. Organisms that are too similar or too far apart die out.

Example:
  evoltree

mandelbrot.description: An interactive visualization of the Mandelbrot set.
mandelbrot.details:
A fractal viewer. Use the mouse wheel or pinch-to-zoom to explore. Click and drag to pan.

Example:
  mandelbrot

gravitysim.details:
Simulates a galaxy of particles interacting under a simplified gravity model. The mouse acts as a massive 'sun' that attracts particles.

Example:
  gravitysim

glideroflife.description: A survival game based on Conway's Game of Life.
glideroflife.details:
Control a single glider with the arrow keys. Navigate the grid to collect food for points while avoiding disruption by other 'wild' cells. Collecting food increases the game speed and spawns more wild cells.

Example:
  glideroflife

gliderpong.description: A physics-based Pong game using paper gliders. Inspired by Paperfall.
gliderpong.details:
A multi-mode game in which paper gliders deflect a bouncing ball. Gliders are governed by realistic aerodynamic physics: gravity, air resistance, and a rising wind zone near the bottom of the arena. The ball speeds up with each hit.

Modes (select on the title screen):
  1  Single Player  Deflect the ball and clear all bricks
  2  Versus         Two players on one keyboard; last glider standing wins
  3  Practice       Ball bounces off the floor; drag the WIND tab to adjust wind height

Controls:
  P1 (or single):  Arrow Keys, or tap screen halves on mobile
  P2:              A / D
  Space / Enter:   Start or restart
  Escape:          Pause / return to title

Example:
  gliderpong

wordweaver.description: A word game where you navigate a latent language space to reach a target phrase.
wordweaver.details:
An NLP typing game powered by an n-gram language model. Starting from a seed phrase, you build a sentence one word at a time, guided by the model's top grammatical predictions.

Goal: reach the TARGET SEQUENCE shown at the top before you run out of valid paths.

Rules:
  - You may only type words that appear in the prediction candidates shown in brackets.
  - Words highlighted in blue lie on the hidden optimal path to the target.
  - Only the top 10% of predicted candidates are valid (minimum 5).

Controls:
  Tab              Autocomplete to the common prefix; or commit if the word is already complete
  Enter            Commit the current word
  Ctrl+Backspace   Undo the last committed word

The seed input on the menu screen pins the puzzle — share a seed to challenge a friend to the same game.

Example:
  wordweaver

eliza.description: A simulation of a Rogerian psychotherapist.
eliza.details:
A JavaScript port of Joseph Weizenbaum's classic 1966 ELIZA program. It engages in a text-based conversation by parodying a non-directional psychotherapist.

Example:
  eliza

radio.description: An infinite procedural chiptune radio station and visualizer. The generated music is synchronized across all clients in real-time.
radio.details:
Generates real-time 8-bit music using algorithmic composition and chiptune synthesis.

Subcommands/Arguments:
  id            Displays the current station ID and copies it to the clipboard.
  [number]      Tunes to a specific station ID and stays on that song.

Examples:
  radio
  radio id
  radio 42

stream.description: A synchronous generative text broadcast based on n-gram language modeling (trained on books I like).
stream.details:
When run without arguments, connects to a globally synchronized timeline where all users observe the exact same procedural text being generated simultaneously.

When provided with text arguments, detaches from the global broadcast and generates a single custom block continuing from your prompt.

Examples:
  stream
  stream the ancient forest

fractal.description: A synchronized procedural fractal visualizer that morphs between formulas over time.
fractal.details:
Renders an evolving GPU-accelerated fractal in real time, shared across all viewers. Seamlessly blends between Mandelbrot, Burning Ship, and higher-order power variants on a 60-second cycle.

The interior of the set uses orbit-trap coloring to produce nebula-like structures. No interaction required.

Example:
  fractal

tv.description: A composite audio-visual broadcast: synchronized fractals, chiptune music, and generative subtitles with speech synthesis.
tv.details:
Combines three programs into a single ambient broadcast:
  - Visuals:   Evolving fractals (fractal)
  - Audio:     Generative chiptune music (radio)
  - Subtitles: Deterministic n-gram text (stream), read aloud via browser TTS

All layers are globally synchronized — every viewer sees and hears the same broadcast at the same moment.

Example:
  tv

img.description: Displays a random image based on a specified topic.
img.details:
Fetches a high-resolution random image from LoremFlickr. If no topic is provided, it defaults to 'nature'. Supports multi-word topics for specific searches.

Examples:
  img
  img deep space
  img brutalist architecture

stats.description: Displays comprehensive system, hardware, and session diagnostics.
stats.details:
Aggregates all available browser telemetry, including hardware specs (CPU cores, RAM estimate, GPU renderer), display metrics (resolution, refresh rate, HDR support), network performance, and persistent visit counts.

Example:
  stats

sun.description: Displays live high-resolution imagery of the Sun from NASA's SDO.
sun.details:
Provides real-time solar observations from the Solar Dynamics Observatory. Hovering over the image toggles between the Photosphere (visible light) and the Corona (Extreme Ultraviolet, 171Å).

Example:
  sun

nasa.description: Displays NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day.
nasa.details:
Fetches the daily featured image or video from NASA. The media fills the console view; scroll down to read the professional astronomer's explanation. Clicking the image opens the high-definition version in a new tab.

Example:
  nasa

ip.description: Displays information about an IP address.
ip.details:
Retrieves network and geolocation data. If no address is provided, it returns data for your current public IP. Otherwise, it looks up the specified IPv4 or IPv6 address.

Examples:
  ip
  ip 8.8.8.8

read.description: Reads and renders a file from the data directory.
read.details:
Fetches and displays a file from the data/sections/ or data/ directories. Supports Markdown (.md), plain text (.txt), HTML (.html), and JSON (.json). Markdown is rendered with formatting; plain text is shown verbatim.

Examples:
  read quotes
  read games

txt.description: Text processing utilities: transform, analyze, or clean pasted text.
txt.details:
Applies a text transformation to the supplied input. For all subcommands except 'stats', the result is automatically copied to the clipboard.

Subcommands:
  lower   Convert to lowercase
  title   Convert to Title Case
  clean   Strip all non-alphanumeric characters
  sort    Clean and sort words alphabetically
  stats   Show word count, top words, bigrams, and trigrams
  urls    Extract all URLs from the text

Examples:
  txt lower Hello World
  txt stats The quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog
  txt urls Visit https://example.com and https://nasa.gov

chat.description: A decentralized P2P chat room over WebRTC.
chat.details:
Connects to a global peer-to-peer chat room with no central server. Uses WebRTC for direct browser-to-browser communication. Enter a username to join.

Commands (once connected):
  /users   List all currently connected users
  /quit    Disconnect and leave the room

Example:
  chat
